speaker: interviewer
text: Irene Zisblatt, tape 6. Irene, we had reached the point where you were on the wagon and you had arrived in a camp in the Sudeten. Can we back track just to finish the story you began with the Kanada, where you were asked to deliver something to two women in the camp, in the C lager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure. What I delivered was a gold bracelet that was rolled up in a little tiny thing. It was like links. And I did manage to deliver it to the three sisters that were in my-- in my camp. And this was a fourth sister of theirs that sent that. And they were taken away the next day to a labor camp. And yes, they are alive today. They managed to save their lives with that gold bracelet that I delivered. And they live in Australia. And they have families. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they-- how did the gold bracelet figure in saving their lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: They bought their-- their way out of hunger, I think. And sickness. They didn't escape of any camp or anything. But they were able to buy food, and-- and I don't know what else it gave them. But they are alive today, and they say because of that that-- that they-- they are alive today before I was-- because I was able to give them that. And also in Birkenau, when my cousin died and her sister remained, she also-- she just died two years ago-- but she came. When I came to this country and she was already here, she lived in Lakewood, New Jersey. And I lived in-- she found out that I was alive about-- in the '50s. She didn't know. I came to this country in 1947. And until-- in the '50s, she didn't know that I was alive. And when she found out, she came to thank me for sharing my last piece of soup with her. Because I used to give her my sips of soup and so she can live. And so she felt that that's what saved her life. And she-- she survived also. Unfortunately, she is dead today. She died of cancer in Lakewood, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where had she been transported to when she was selected out of your lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was selected out of Birkenau and sent to-- I think she said she was working in an ammunition factory in Mauthausen. I'm not sure which camp. But she was in a labor camp after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get back to your arrival at the camp in Sudeten--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived there, we were assigned to bunks, our own bunks. And I thought I was in a hotel because the bunks had straw, and a blanket, and pillow. And I said, no, this is not for us. This is a impossible. What-- what did we do to deserve this? But yes, it was. We were supposed to be mechanics to fix the machines that-- that were in the factory. And these were our quarters. And we were brought in at night so everybody was sleeping. It was a very small camp because I only remember seeing one huge building. And I don't know, maybe there were buildings behind that one. But I only-- only-- I always only saw this building because we were taking each morning and go across a street into the factory, and stayed there all day and worked, and came back to the same building at night. So I don't know what else went on there. But the-- but the best part of this-- of-- of-- being there was that I found my friend, Sabka again. I woke up the next morning, and she was in the bunk underneath me. I was in the middle, and she was in the first bunk underneath me. And I put my head down to see whether there was a real person underneath me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she picked her head up and the two of us met. You know, we looked at each other, and at the same time we both screamed. How-- how did you get here? And I was yelling, what are you doing here? And she just jumped out of bed and pulled me off the-- the bunk and-- and-- we hugged and kissed. And I said, Sabka, what are we doing? What are we gonna do here? And she says, it's OK. It's OK. It's good here. It's good here. You're gonna work. You're gonna eat. You're gonna have clothes. They're not gonna shave your hair. You'll just be a good worker. She says, what are you? I says, a mechanic. I'm supposed to be a mechanic. And she says, just be a good mechanic, and-- and that's all. Don't be afraid. It's good. It's-- it's the best you're ever gonna see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, and it was but unfortunately not for long. And we worked. We didn't work together, but we saw each other every night when we came home. And-- and we were able to talk without being afraid of being separated again. And-- and we just promised each other that we will never go away with-- without one another. And thank god we didn't have to after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work that you did there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the mechanic that I was supposed to be. And of course, I didn't know anything about being a mechanic. But in the factory, we didn't work the machines. Old men worked the machines that weren't eligible to go to the army. And they were Czech. They were not German or Jewish. They were Czech, non-Jewish people. But they were old. They were-- to me, they were ancient. They must have been in their 50s, or 60s, or maybe older. And they were the machine operators. And we were supposed to fix the machines if something went wrong with them. And so each one of us were assigned to a machine. I was working with a man, with an old man-- like he could have been my grandfather. And we weren't allowed to talk to them or anything. We were just supposed to do what they tell us to do. And he was watching me for a whole day the first day. They-- they gave him my number that if anything you know to call my number. And I sat there all day watching. And he had me sometimes pick up some things around like scraps of iron and metal that was from certain things from the machine that I put together. And-- but other than that he wasn't talking to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next day, he started to mumble in Czech. He-- he was talking to himself. But I understood Czech at that time. So I-- I was listening. And he was complaining that why are they sending him a child to do a man's work? And what is this world coming to, and all of that stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I said, I don't know how to fix this machine. And he said, I didn't think that you knew anything. But he says, I am going to show you what to do with your tool box. Because when the Gestapo comes to check, and they gonna ask you what to do with your-- with your stuff, and if you don't know, they're gonna send you to Auschwitz. And I said, OK. And so he opened up my tool box, and he took out a hammer. And he says, this is a hammer and this is what you do with it. And this is the pliers and this-- and he showed me on the machine. If this breaks, you take out the pliers, and this piece, and you-- you do this. And he says, I'm not gonna let you do it. I'll do it. But in case they come and they-- they want you to do it, you do it because you will go back. You'll go-- he didn't say, back. He didn't know I came from there. You'll go to Auschwitz. They send everybody to Auschwitz if they doesn't do what they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and I didn't make any mention. And then he started to talk to me after that every day. You know, he says, do you know what Auschwitz is? And I said, yeah. And he says, who told you? And I says, well, I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, oh, you have to tell me. And I was afraid to tell him. And I said, it's-- it's true whatever you know. It's-- whatever you know about Auschwitz, it's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: And didn't tell him anything extra. You know, I didn't know-- I was afraid. And then he brought me a piece of salami one day. It was like two bites. You know? And I said-- he says, I'm sorry I can't bring you more, but we don't have much either.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day he would bring me a bite of bread. And-- one day he brought me like something cooked. Like-- and I wouldn't eat that because I was very hungry, but I was afraid that it's gonna-- it's gonna kill me or something. And I said, I'll just eat the bread, you know. And I ate the bread. Because I remember in school we learned like if you're-- if you're-- one of the papers I remember I had to do in school is when if you're stranded on an island without food and water for a long time. And then when you come back to civilization, what to eat to be safe. So I remember that you were only supposed to drink and have something that will dissolve in your stomach like stale bread or-- or something like that. So I just ate the bread because I didn't know that was. And I mean, I wanted to eat it. I was so hungry. But I-- then I said, I came so far. I'm-- I'm gonna try not to eat it. So I ate the bread. And-- and then we worked there for a very short time. But it wasn't bad. We had an infirmary. If-- if we were sick, we can go and get an aspirin or something. And-- and we had-- we were able to take-- like wash ourselves. We had like a bathroom with sinks so we can go wash up. We had the same clothes we wore every day. We didn't get new clothing. But we were able to take it off and air it out, and put it back on, and wash our body. And we-- we felt a little bit more human. And then the invasion came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in January. It was the first week in January, because I remember seeing the Christmas trees in the offices of the Gestapo. And they were still on. And it said, you know, in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means happy new year. And so it was like the first week in January. And d and then the invasion came. And people came into this factory, where-- to this camp of the factory from other parts of the area. A lot of people were digging trenches for the soldiers in the fields. Those people were-- they were already gathered together to go on the death march. So they came into-- to us one night. And then the next morning, there was 5,000 of us. The whole camp only had a couple of hundred people there. But then the next morning, we-- we were out on appel and they counted 5,000 people. They started us out on the march. The snow was this high. We-- we had our shoes. We had normal shoes. We had-- we took the blanket. We weren't allowed to take anything else but the blanket and whatever we had on our body. We took our blanket. We had normal shoes. The people that came to us, they were grabbing up everything they could from us because they had nothing. And we started on the march. And the march was like back to square one. They were pushing, and they were yelling, and they were hitting. And they-- and whoever couldn't keep up, they shot them on the side of the road and left them laying in the snow. And they pushed us on and on and on. And the whole winter, we were like. We never saw anything green. We never-- they never gave us food. We ate the snow. As we walked by, we grabbed a handful of snow. We were eating that from January until April. April was no more snow. So we had nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your route? The route that you took?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember passing a town called Breslau and Dresden. And-- and that's the main thing. I remember the signs as we were going. Now I know it was Sudetendeutschland because we were at the factory. It was somewhere near deep, deep Germany. And that-- but we were coming-- we were passing Dresden and-- which was below where we were. So we didn't pay attention where we were going. We just had no choice anyway. But by April, we were somewhere in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the Pilsen area. And we knew that we were close to Czechoslovakia-- that used to be Czechoslovakia anyway. And we were-- we were just marching and marching. One night they-- they gave us-- we passed a town and there were a lot of farms in that town. The snow-- this was still like in January, February. And they-- they led us into a farm. By then we were only about maybe 2,000 people because the rest they killed on the side of the road that couldn't keep up. And they led us in-- onto this farm. And they let us in through the farm-- like the stalls where they used to have the-- it was-- it was empty. They just had a lot of hay in there. And so they had like two stories of this-- this-- this-- this-- this building. And so they let us spend the night there. Like the farmer told them that we could spent the night there. And so they led us in there into that building. And they locked us in there, of course. And inside at least there was hay so we were warm. And up on the second floor, the farmer, they stored fruits and vegetables for the winter. So it was all packed in and covered with hay so it doesn't freeze. Well, one of the inmates managed to get up there and found the food. Because, see, everybody was always scavenging for what they-- what they can-- what we can eat. And they found all this food. But he-- he-- she-- she came down with all this food and closed up the entrance where-- where she went up. And there was another door to go up. But it was small, and she couldn't fit in there. Because the-- the rest of the food was I think on the other side. I don't know how she figured it out. But she came down with all this food and of course there was a panic down there. Everybody wanted food. So there wasn't enough for everybody. So everybody was trying to get a piece of food. So there was a lot of noise, a lot of commotion. But they found this other door. And it was-- and I was like small so they helped me up there. And I went up there, and I was throwing down whatever I can find. Potatoes and-- and whatever was there. I just kept on throwing down that hole. Because I knew I couldn't take down enough for everybody. And there was such a noise going on there already. They were fighting whatever was down there. I just kept throwing everything down. And the noise was so bad, the doors opened and they caught me. They caught me up there. And all these people fighting about the food. And this Gestapo pulled me off. He-- he-- you know, from the top. And he-- I fell down from the second floor down on the-- but it was snow. So, you know, And he hit me. I still have a mark today on my head. He hit me. He beat me up so bad with the back of his rifle. And my head-- my head was bleeding. It was the middle of the night. It was cold. It was freezing, and left me there to die. And he says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he walked away. And that's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next morning, I found myself walking. Two people were dragging me. And one of them was my friend, Sabka. And I said, where are we, and why are you dragging me, and why didn't you leave me to die? I was dead already. And she says, you're not gonna die. I don't want you to die. You have to live. And I says, why did they beat me? I didn't remember anything, you know. She says, because we-- you were feeding us. So they found out and caught you. And she told me the whole story. And she says, you can't die. You can't die. I says, Sabka, you want to die, and you're telling me that you don't want me to die? She says, look, we're not gonna become ashes. All I want you to remember, please don't let them make me become ashes. I don't care if I die. But today, you don't die. So they dragged me. I couldn't-- I didn't care. I wanted to die. I says, leave me here. Let them shoot me. I-- I couldn't take it. I was-- I was just like so desperate to get out of this. And she held me up. She really held me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went on and on. And in April, there was no snow so we had nothing to eat. And we found ourselves in-- on a road between two-- it was woods and forests. Nothing else but this country road with a lot of wood on each side of us. And we were just marching. All these few people hundred people. A lot of trucks that-- from our transport with the SS with their supplies, and ammunition, one motorcycle, and a couple of trucks, and the guards walking alongside of us with their machine guns and stuff to guard us. And all of a sudden on this nice, bright, sunny afternoon, planes came out from behind those woods. And they just bombed the whole transport. The trucks, and the motorcycles, and everything that moved they bombed. They-- we like ran off the road onto the-- to the side of the road. They wouldn't let us go into the woods. But just on the side of the road so they can pass with their equipment, the-- the Nazis. But they got them. They didn't get us. Nobody of our people died from the bomb or the shooting from the planes. They just shot up their-- those trucks. They turned around and left. These planes. So they either were Russian, or French, or American, or one of the Allies. We-- to the very day, we don't know which ones it was. But they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So now they are wounded. They have their own problems. And we are on the side of the road. And there's the-- the-- the foot soldiers-- the SS that were guarding us. They scooped us all up and we kept on. They kept on taking us. And the trucks and everything that was bombed that was left behind. A couple of the SS got wounded. They turned around. And they on the motorcycle, and whatever was movable, they turned around and they went back from where we came from. And they kept marching us on. So we-- that night, we were still on the same road. And dark. There wasn't a star in heaven. And all these woods in this country road. And then there is a jeep and a truck coming from the opposite direction with bright, bright lights. And they stopped in front of us. They made-- they yelled-- they were Germans. They were German officers. And they yelled for the-- for the leader of the transport. So the leader went up to them and we heard him say, what-- why are you taking these Jews? Where are you-- why are you so busy with these Jews? The war is over. You better-- you better go and find a place to hide because the Allies are around the corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these guards said, that-- they-- they-- we are going to Theresienstadt to [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, to destroy them and to the destruction camp. I was never in Theresienstadt so I didn't know what was going on in Theresienstadt. But when he said, [GERMAN], to destroy us, I just imagined a whole bunch of crematoriums and gas chambers. And they're gonna take us there and they're going to make ashes out of us. And that's one thing Sabka and I didn't want is to become ashes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a blanket. And she had a blanket. And she had a white blanket. And I had a gray blanket. And I said, Sabka, you have to throw away your blanket because it's gonna reflect in the dark. And she says, look this is the only thing that I have in my possession, and I'm not giving it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, take my blanket. I gave her my blanket. We threw away her blanket, and I yanked her off the road. And we went down on the ditch, and we laid in that ditch, you know, on the side of the road until everybody passed. And then we got up, and we looked around. We couldn't see anything. We didn't know where we were. But I said, let's just walk up a little way and see if there is something that-- someplace we can hide until daylight. So we can see what to do. And we walked up and we saw a bridge. So we went under the bridge. We figured we'll stay until daybreak. But on under the bridge was a whole factory, ammunition factory. There were lights on, and there were piles of bullets and stuff, and-- and pieces-- parts of, you know, rifles. And she says, we better get out of here. There might be Germans here. And we were recognizable in a minute because we had no hair. We had striped clothes. And so we ran back up on the road. And we didn't want to go this way because that's where everybody went to be destroyed. And we couldn't go back because that's where all the wounded are, and they're gonna find us. So we decided we'll just go into deep into the woods. And just keep on going into the woods. And that's what we did. And all night long, we just walked and walked and walked. And there was no end to these woods. But we got so tired. And we found a little stream of water coming down the mountain. So we just rested there and we drank some water. And-- and then we went up again a little ways and we found like an open field. So we figured, well, maybe this was a farm or something. Let's just dig in the ground. Maybe we can find something to eat like leftover things that they didn't harvest. Like potatoes, or turnips, or whatever. And sure enough we found a potato. One potato. And we shared the potato. And we back to the-- to the stream where the water was. And with the one blanket, we covered ourselves up and we went to sleep. And I don't know how long we slept. Whether it was a day or a-- or a five days. But all of a sudden, we felt like something poking us. And they poked her first because she-- she-- she-- you know, she says, I told you they're gonna find us. They found us. Somebody is poking me with the back of a rifle. I says, well, let's find out who is poking you. And she-- and then they started to poke me, too. And I says, well, let's find out. She says, I don't want to see when they're gonna shoot me. If you want to see how they're gonna shoot you, then you go and see. I said, well if you let go of the blanket maybe I can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [LAUGHS] she let go of the blanket. And I picked up the blanket a little bit and I saw two sets of feet. Boots. Big, big feet from boots. Of course, to me anything was so big, you know? And big boots. And I said, well, it's not German boots. She says, so what difference? They disguise their feet. I says, Sabka, I says, I don't think so. I says, I don't think they're going to shoot us. Maybe they-- they're gonna do something else to us. But I says, I don't know. But they're not Germans. They don't have the boots like the Germans had to have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the color is different of the pants. The pants are green. And the Germans have black and brown. And not like that. She says, look, I don't want to see nothing. They're gonna take us back to the camp. But anyway, I uncovered this blanket. I pulled the blanket off. And I looked up and I kept looking up. They were so tall. And their eyes were bulging when they saw me. They didn't know whether I was human or I was-- they-- and they started to talk among themselves. And I couldn't understand. And I spoke seven languages at that time. And I said to Sabka, they don't speak like we do. I don't understand them. She says, yeah, I can hear. She says, they sound like they have hot potatoes in their mouth. [LAUGHS] And I says, let's ask them who they are. And she says, you better not ask anything. And then they started to kind of try to communicate with us. But I couldn't understand them. So I was trying to. Like, we were-- I says, Sabka, come out and help me out. And she still was very much-- you know, she was at the-- she was sick already, but see, I didn't know she was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- she came out and between the two of us we-- we-- we tried with our arms, with our hands, too. And one of the soldiers got so frustrated he opened up his shirt and his dog tags hung out. And on the dog tags he had a mezuzah. Well, I just jumped up in his chest. And I grabbed the mezuzah. And he says, you know what that is, like? And I says, a mezuzah, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he peeled me off his chest, and he put me down on the ground. And he motioned to stay there. And him and this other guy, they left. And in a few minutes, I hear all this rumbling, and noise, and wagons, and-- and I mean cars, and footsteps. And their whole army was here. And they came down and they found the two of us. And they had an interpreter. So we communicated. And we explained who we are. They didn't know. They didn't see-- we were the first survivors out of a camp. That this was General Patton's Third Army that came through there. And they-- they didn't understand what we were telling them. But-- And this one guy was always screaming. And-- and you know, that was General Patton. He was screaming and yelling. And then the Red Cross truck came down. And they put us in the truck. And they gave us water and-- to wash up. To-- and they gave-- and then they wanted to give us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they asked us what we want to eat? And Sabka, my Sabka, she only wanted scrambled eggs. All the five years she was in camp, she says, I just want to live to have scrambled eggs one time. And I just wanted to live to have long enough to have one small loaf of bread to myself. So I don't have to share it with 40 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so our dreams came true. They gave us a loaf of bread. And they were making her scrambled eggs. And she ate the scrambled eggs. And I had my bread and not to have to share. And they put us in the Red Cross truck, they-- on cots. You know, we felt like we were in the best hotel in the whole world. We felt so human and so grateful. And so, we couldn't communicate with them, but we-- we were just like-- they were just God to us. Like God sent these angels down to us. [CRYING] And they got us out of our misery. And then they-- they took us into the hospital in Pilsen after they scooped us up. And they cleaned us up. And that night in the Red Cross truck, Sabka and I talked about everything imaginable.
